          DuckDB is not a replacement for MySQL or PostgreSQL. It's a different tool for a different job: local analytical queries instead of multi-user transactional applications.
        
      
    

    
    
      
        Querying files directly — no database needed
        
-- Query a Parquet file directly on disk
SELECT
  column_a,
  SUM(column_b) AS total
FROM read_parquet('data.parquet')
WHERE column_a = 'value'
GROUP BY column_a;
-- Query ALL parquet files in a folder
SELECT * FROM read_parquet('folder/*.parquet');
-- Load into a persistent DuckDB database
CREATE TABLE stops AS
  SELECT * FROM read_parquet('*.parquet');
        
        
          DuckDB reads the Parquet footer first, pushes predicates into the scan, and skips irrelevant row groups.
        
      
    

  

  

    
      3.1 · What is DuckDB
      An embedded SQL engine and no server required
      DuckDB is described as "the SQLite for analytics." SQLite is the local SQL dialect and like SQLite, DuckDB is an embedded, in-process database. It runs entirely inside your program rather than as a separate server. The entire engine is a single library you install as a package in Python or R, though DuckDB also ships a standalone CLI (command-line interface) you can run directly from the terminal without any host language. There is no authentication, no port to configure, and no connection to start.
      Unlike SQLite, which is optimized for transactional row-level operations just like MySQL, DuckDB is purpose-built for analytical workloads. It uses a columnar execution engine, processes data in batches, and is optimized for queries that scan many rows, aggregate, group, filter, and join large datasets.
      A DuckDB database is a single file on disk with a .duckdb extension. You can also use DuckDB in fully in-memory mode, where nothing is written to disk at all. Both modes give you full SQL expressiveness with analytical performance.
      
        Embedded / in-process
        No server needed
        Columnar execution
        Single-file database
      
    

    
      3.2 · DuckDB vs. Traditional SQL
      A different kind of SQL database
      MySQL and PostgreSQL are client-server databases: a long-running server process manages shared access, handles concurrent transactions, and stores data in row-oriented storage. This architecture is excellent for web applications with many simultaneous users reading and writing small numbers of rows.
      DuckDB makes the opposite trade-offs. It is designed for a single analyst or process running complex queries over large datasets. Because it runs in-process, there is no network overhead between the query engine and the query. The results come back as in-memory data frames, not network packets like with MySQL or PostgreSQL.
      The storage engine is also fundamentally different. MySQL stores table data in B+ trees (row-oriented). DuckDB stores data columnarly with vectorized execution, processing columns in batches of 2,048 values at a time. This is precisely what makes aggregation queries fast: the CPU can operate on contiguous arrays of values rather than jumping between rows.
      
        Vectorized execution
        No network overhead
        Batch processing
        Single-user analytics
      
    

    
      3.3 · Querying Files Directly
      DuckDB can query Parquet files without loading them first
      One of DuckDB's most powerful features is the ability to run SQL queries directly against parquet files on disk, no database ingestion step required. The read_parquet() function accepts a file path (or a glob pattern like *.parquet) and exposes it as a queryable table.
      This is not just a convenience feature. DuckDB's Parquet reader exploits the file's footer statistics to implement predicate pushdown, the same row group skipping described earlier. A filter in your SQL WHERE clause is pushed all the way into the file scan, skipping row groups that can't match. This means you get analytical warehouse behavior on local files.
      When you do want persistence, the CREATE TABLE AS SELECT pattern copies data into DuckDB's own columnar format, which is even faster for repeated queries. The choice between querying files directly and loading into a native database is a trade-off depending on the query and the frequency of the each type of querying.
      
        read_parquet()
        Glob patterns
        Predicate pushdown
        CTAS for persistence
      
    

  






  Key Insights
  
    
      01
      Storage layout determines query performance. Row-based formats (CSV, JSON) store each record together: good for returning full rows, slow for scanning one column across millions. Columnar formats store each column together: excellent for analytical aggregations, less ideal for full-row retrieval.
    
    
      02
      Parquet is a hybrid: rows are partitioned into large row groups, and within each group data is stored columnarly. This gives both compression efficiency and fast columnar scans. The footer stores schema and per-row-group statistics enabling predicate pushdown: row groups are skipped before any data is read.
    
    
      03
      Parquet compresses dramatically better than CSV or JSON due to columnar grouping (similar values together) and dictionary encoding (repeating strings replaced with compact integer codes). The same dataset can be 3–10× smaller as Parquet, which means faster queries because less data is read from disk.
    
    
      04
      DuckDB is an in-process analytical SQL database, install as a package, query immediately. A database is a single .duckdb file. It uses vectorized, columnar execution and processes data in batches of 2,048 values, making it fast for aggregation-heavy analytical queries.
    
    
      05
      Once ingested into DuckDB, the source format is irrelevant. DuckDB normalizes CSV, Parquet, and JSON into its own internal columnar representation. Query performance converges regardless of source once source is uploaded into DuckDB.
    
    
      06
      Native DuckDB and direct Parquet are both columnar but not the same. Parquet is a file format read on demand from disk. DuckDB ingests data into its own internal columnar storage. Once inside, the source format no longer matters. The trade-off is storage (DuckDB takes ~3× more space) versus speed (DuckDB is faster for repeated queries). Click here to see an exploration on Native DuckDB and direct Parquet Querying.

title: Parquet Querying Time Exploration
text:
This page reports the results of two controlled benchmarking experiments examining how DuckDB query performance changes as a function of (1) the number of Parquet files queried and (2) the total number of rows in those files. Both experiments use DuckDB’s read_parquet() function to query files directly from disk without loading them into a native DuckDB database first. For background on Parquet’s internal structure and how DuckDB reads Parquet files, see the Informational Resource on File Formats, Parquet & DuckDB..
Two query types are benchmarked throughout:

COUNT(*) — counts every row; requires minimal data I/O but must open and process every file and row within each file.
SELECT c5 — reads a single column across all rows; I/O-intensive but benefits from columnar storage.

Each timing result is the mean of 100 timed runs (Test 1) or 25 timed runs (Test 2), preceded by 5 warmup runs and one additional cache-primer run that is discarded, to ensure stable measurements.

title: Parquet Querying Time Exploration
section: Test 1: Fixed Row Count, Varying File Count
text:
Test 1: Fixed Row Count, Varying File Count

Research Question
When the total number of rows is held constant, does splitting that data across more Parquet files increase query time?


Setup
The first code chunk loads all R packages needed for the experiment, including duckdb for the database engine, ggplot2 and scales for plotting, and glue for string interpolation in SQL queries.


Show code
library(duckdb)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(glue)


Next, we must define all experiment parameters in one place: the total row count, the file split sizes to test, the number of warmup and timed runs, and the directory where Parquet files will be written. Keeping these here makes the experiment easy to re-run with different settings by changing only this chunk.


Show code
TOTAL_ROWS   <- 1000000
SPLITS       <- c(1, 4, 10, 20, 50, 75, 100, 200)
WARMUP_RUNS  <- 5
TIMED_RUNS   <- 100
PARQUET_DIR  <- file.path(tempdir(), "parquet_splits")


One million rows are written to disk, split evenly across varying numbers of Parquet files (1 through 200). Every configuration contains exactly the same data(incrementing integers); only the number of files differs. Each file contains ten integer columns (c1 through c10).


Show code
con <- dbConnect(duckdb())

dbExecute(con, glue::glue("
  CREATE TABLE base AS
  SELECT
    row_number() OVER () AS c1,
    row_number() OVER () AS c2,
    row_number() OVER () AS c3,
    row_number() OVER () AS c4,
    row_number() OVER () AS c5,
    row_number() OVER () AS c6,
    row_number() OVER () AS c7,
    row_number() OVER () AS c8,
    row_number() OVER () AS c9,
    row_number() OVER () AS c10
  FROM range({as.integer(TOTAL_ROWS)})
"))


The code chunk above opens an in-memory DuckDB connection and creates a base table of one million rows with ten integer columns, where every value in each column is its row number. This table is the single source of truth that all Parquet splits will be derived from.
The code chunk below loops through each file count in SPLITS and writes the base table to disk as that many evenly-sized Parquet files using DuckDB’s COPY command. Each split gets its own subfolder, so for example the 50-file configuration writes 50 .parquet files each containing 20,000 rows.


Show code
dir.create(PARQUET_DIR, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

for (n_files in SPLITS) {
  folder <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, sprintf("split_%03d", n_files))
  dir.create(folder, showWarnings = FALSE)
  rows_per_file <- TOTAL_ROWS %/% n_files

  for (i in seq_len(n_files)) {
    offset <- (i - 1) * rows_per_file
    limit  <- if (i < n_files) rows_per_file else TOTAL_ROWS - offset
    path   <- file.path(folder, sprintf("part_%04d.parquet", i))
    dbExecute(con, sprintf(
      "COPY (SELECT * FROM base LIMIT %d OFFSET %d) TO '%s' (FORMAT PARQUET)",
      limit, offset, path
    ))
  }
}




Benchmark
Next we define a bench_query() function that runs a given SQL query a fixed number of warmup times (discarded) then a fixed number of timed times, returning the mean elapsed time in milliseconds. It then applies this function to both a COUNT(*) query and a SELECT c5 query across each Parquet file split, collecting the results into a single data frame.


Show code
bench_query <- function(con, sql, warmup = WARMUP_RUNS, timed = TIMED_RUNS) {
  for (i in seq_len(warmup)) dbGetQuery(con, sql)
  dbGetQuery(con, sql)
  times_ms <- numeric(timed)
  for (i in seq_len(timed)) {
    t0 <- proc.time()[["elapsed"]]
    dbGetQuery(con, sql)
    times_ms[i] <- (proc.time()[["elapsed"]] - t0) * 1000
  }
  mean(times_ms)
}

results <- lapply(SPLITS, function(n_files) {
  glob <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, sprintf("split_%03d", n_files), "*.parquet")
  data.frame(
    n_files   = n_files,
    count_ms  = bench_query(con, sprintf("SELECT count(*) FROM read_parquet('%s')", glob)),
    select_ms = bench_query(con, sprintf("SELECT c5 FROM read_parquet('%s')", glob))
  )
})

results_df <- bind_rows(results)




Results


Show code
results_df |>
  mutate(
    `Files`          = n_files,
    `COUNT(*) (ms)`  = round(count_ms, 2),
    `SELECT c5 (ms)` = round(select_ms, 2)
  ) |>
  select(`Files`, `COUNT(*) (ms)`, `SELECT c5 (ms)`) |>
  knitr::kable(align = "r", caption = "Mean query time by file count (1,000,000 rows fixed)")



Mean query time by file count (1,000,000 rows fixed)

Show code
ggplot(results_df, aes(x = factor(n_files), y = count_ms)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#185FA5", width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f ms", count_ms)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.2, colour = "#185FA5") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, " ms"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Number of Parquet files",
    y = "Mean time (ms)",
    title = "COUNT(*) query time by file count",
    subtitle = "1,000,000 total rows held constant"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())





Mean COUNT(*) time as file count increases. Total rows held constant at 1,000,000.






Show code
ggplot(results_df, aes(x = factor(n_files), y = select_ms)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#0F6E56", width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f ms", select_ms)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3.2, colour = "#0F6E56") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(x, " ms"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18)),
    limits = c(0, max(results_df$select_ms) * 1.2)
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Number of Parquet files",
    y = "Mean time (ms)",
    title = "SELECT c5 query time by file count",
    subtitle = "1,000,000 total rows held constant"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())





Mean SELECT c5 time as file count increases. Total rows held constant at 1,000,000.






Show code
scale_factor <- max(results_df$select_ms) / max(results_df$count_ms)

results_long <- results_df |>
  mutate(select_ms_scaled = select_ms / scale_factor) |>
  pivot_longer(c(count_ms, select_ms_scaled),
               names_to = "query", values_to = "ms") |>
  mutate(query = recode(query,
    count_ms         = "COUNT(*)",
    select_ms_scaled = "SELECT c5"
  ))

ggplot(results_long, aes(x = factor(n_files), y = ms,
                          colour = query, group = query)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("COUNT(*)" = "#185FA5", "SELECT c5" = "#0F6E56")) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name     = "COUNT(*) mean time (ms)",
    labels   = function(x) paste0(round(x, 1), " ms"),
    sec.axis = sec_axis(
      transform = ~ . * scale_factor,
      name      = "SELECT c5 mean time (ms)",
      labels    = function(x) paste0(round(x), " ms")
    )
  ) +
  labs(
    x      = "Number of Parquet files",
    colour = NULL,
    title  = "COUNT(*) vs SELECT c5 — file count overhead",
    subtitle = "1,000,000 rows held constant across all splits"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank())





Both query types plotted together. Dual y-axes account for the difference in absolute times between COUNT(*) and SELECT c5.






Interpretation
COUNT(*) shows a clear and consistent increase with file count even though the total number of rows never changes. This is attributable to per-file overhead: DuckDB must open, parse the footer of, and coordinate each Parquet file individually before aggregating the result. When the data is stored in a single file this overhead is incurred once; at 200 files it is incurred 200 times. Since COUNT(*) requires minimal data I/O — DuckDB can satisfy the command from footer statistics alone — this per-file cost is the dominant factor.
SELECT c5 is less sensitive to file count at low file numbers, because the cost of physically reading and decompressing a full column of data is substantially higher than file-opening overhead. At higher file counts (50+), the coordination overhead becomes more visible and the two trends converge. The key distinction is that for I/O-bound queries, data volume matters more than file count; for metadata-bound queries like COUNT(*), file count matters more than data volume.

title: Parquet Querying Time Exploration
section: Test 2: Fixed File Count, Varying Row Count
text:
Test 2: Fixed File Count, Varying Row Count

Research Question
When the number of Parquet files is held constant at 50, how does query time scale with total row count? Does splitting data across multiple files provide any advantage over a single file as row counts grow?


Setup


Show code
ROW_COUNTS  <- c(100, 10000, 100000, 500000, 1000000, 2000000)
N_FILES     <- 100
WARMUP_RUNS <- 5
TIMED_RUNS  <- 50
PARQUET_DIR <- file.path(tempdir(), "parquet_rowtest")


For each row count, the same data is written in two configurations: a single Parquet file containing all rows, and 50 files each containing an equal share of rows. Both configurations hold identical data; only the number of files differs.


Show code
dir.create(PARQUET_DIR, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
con <- dbConnect(duckdb())

for (total_rows in ROW_COUNTS) {
  dbExecute(con, "DROP TABLE IF EXISTS base")
  dbExecute(con, glue("
    CREATE TABLE base AS
    SELECT
      row_number() OVER () AS c1,
      row_number() OVER () AS c2,
      row_number() OVER () AS c3,
      row_number() OVER () AS c4,
      row_number() OVER () AS c5,
      row_number() OVER () AS c6,
      row_number() OVER () AS c7,
      row_number() OVER () AS c8,
      row_number() OVER () AS c9,
      row_number() OVER () AS c10
    FROM range({as.integer(total_rows)})
  "))

  single_dir <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, paste0("single_", total_rows))
  dir.create(single_dir, showWarnings = FALSE)
  dbExecute(con, glue(
    "COPY base TO '{file.path(single_dir, 'data.parquet')}' (FORMAT PARQUET)"
  ))

  multi_dir <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, paste0("multi_", total_rows))
  dir.create(multi_dir, showWarnings = FALSE)
  rows_per_file <- max(1L, as.integer(total_rows) %/% N_FILES)

  for (i in seq_len(N_FILES)) {
    offset <- (i - 1L) * rows_per_file
    limit  <- if (i < N_FILES) rows_per_file else total_rows - offset
    if (limit <= 0) break
    path <- file.path(multi_dir, sprintf("part_%04d.parquet", i))
    dbExecute(con, glue(
      "COPY (SELECT * FROM base LIMIT {limit} OFFSET {offset}) TO '{path}' (FORMAT PARQUET)"
    ))
  }
}




Benchmark
Defines a bench_query() function that runs a given SQL query a fixed number of warmup times (discarded) then a fixed number of timed times, returning the mean elapsed time in milliseconds. It then applies this function to both a COUNT(*) query and a SELECT c5 query across each Parquet file level and row count, collecting the results into a single data frame.


Show code
bench_query2 <- function(con, sql, warmup = WARMUP_RUNS, timed = TIMED_RUNS) {
  for (i in seq_len(warmup)) dbGetQuery(con, sql)
  dbGetQuery(con, sql)
  times_ms <- numeric(timed)
  for (i in seq_len(timed)) {
    t0 <- proc.time()[["elapsed"]]
    dbGetQuery(con, sql)
    times_ms[i] <- (proc.time()[["elapsed"]] - t0) * 1000
  }
  mean(times_ms)
}

results2 <- lapply(ROW_COUNTS, function(total_rows) {
  single_path <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, paste0("single_", total_rows), "data.parquet")
  multi_glob  <- file.path(PARQUET_DIR, paste0("multi_",  total_rows), "*.parquet")
  data.frame(
    total_rows       = total_rows,
    single_count_ms  = bench_query2(con, glue("SELECT count(*) FROM read_parquet('{single_path}')")),
    multi_count_ms   = bench_query2(con, glue("SELECT count(*) FROM read_parquet('{multi_glob}')")),
    single_select_ms = bench_query2(con, glue("SELECT c5 FROM read_parquet('{single_path}')")),
    multi_select_ms  = bench_query2(con, glue("SELECT c5 FROM read_parquet('{multi_glob}')"))
  )
})

results_df2 <- bind_rows(results2)




Results


Show code
results_df2 |>
  mutate(
    `Total rows`            = format(total_rows, big.mark = ","),
    `COUNT — 1 file (ms)`   = round(single_count_ms,  2),
    `COUNT — 50 files (ms)` = round(multi_count_ms,   2),
    `SELECT — 1 file (ms)`  = round(single_select_ms, 2),
    `SELECT — 50 files (ms)`= round(multi_select_ms,  2)
  ) |>
  select(6:10) |>
  knitr::kable(align = "r", caption = "Mean query time by row count (file count fixed at 1 vs 50)")

Show code
count_long2 <- results_df2 |>
  select(total_rows, single_count_ms, multi_count_ms) |>
  pivot_longer(-total_rows, names_to = "condition", values_to = "mean_ms") |>
  mutate(condition = recode(condition,
    single_count_ms = "1 file",
    multi_count_ms  = "50 files"
  ))

ggplot(count_long2, aes(x = total_rows, y = mean_ms,
                         colour = condition, group = condition)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_x_log10(
  labels = function(x) ifelse(x >= 1e6, paste0(x/1e6, "M"),
                       ifelse(x >= 1e3, paste0(x/1e3, "K"), x)),
  breaks = ROW_COUNTS,
  guide  = guide_axis(angle = 45)
) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round(x, 2), " ms")) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("1 file" = "#185FA5", "50 files" = "#A32D2D")) +
  labs(
    x      = "Total rows (log scale)",
    y      = "Mean time (ms)",
    colour = NULL,
    title  = "COUNT(*) — 1 file vs 50 files",
    subtitle = "File count fixed; row count varies"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





COUNT(*) mean time by total row count. The 50-file series consistently exceeds the single-file series due to fixed per-file overhead.






Show code
select_long2 <- results_df2 |>
  select(total_rows, single_select_ms, multi_select_ms) |>
  pivot_longer(-total_rows, names_to = "condition", values_to = "mean_ms") |>
  mutate(condition = recode(condition,
    single_select_ms = "1 file",
    multi_select_ms  = "50 files"
  ))

ggplot(select_long2, aes(x = total_rows, y = mean_ms,
                          colour = condition, group = condition)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_x_log10(
  labels = function(x) ifelse(x >= 1e6, paste0(x/1e6, "M"),
                       ifelse(x >= 1e3, paste0(x/1e3, "K"), x)),
  breaks = ROW_COUNTS,
  guide  = guide_axis(angle = 45)
) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(round(x, 2), " ms")) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("1 file" = "#185FA5", "50 files" = "#0F6E56")) +
  labs(
    x      = "Total rows (log scale)",
    y      = "Mean time (ms)",
    colour = NULL,
    title  = "SELECT c5 — 1 file vs 50 files",
    subtitle = "File count fixed; row count varies"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top", panel.grid.minor = element_blank())





SELECT c5 mean time by total row count. Both series grow together as data volume increases; the gap between them narrows relative to total query time.






Interpretation
COUNT(*) exhibits a stable gap between the single-file and 50-file configurations across all row counts. The absolute difference between the two series changes little as rows increase, confirming that the overhead is driven by file count rather than data volume. At low row counts this overhead represents a large proportion of total query time; at high row counts it becomes relatively smaller but remains present and consistent.
SELECT c5 both series grow together as row count increases because the cost is dominated by reading and decompressing column data, a cost that scales with the number of rows, not the number of files. At larger row counts, the relative gap between the single-file and 50-file configurations shrinks as a proportion of total query time, because the cost of reading and decompressing large volumes of column data becomes the dominant factor and diminishes the relative contribution of per-file overhead.

title: Parquet Querying Time Exploration
text:
Conclusion
Taken together, the two experiments reveal a consistent pattern in how DuckDB handles Parquet files directly from disk. File count and row count impose costs through different mechanisms and affect query types differently.
For COUNT(*), which is the most basic form of a row-based query, file count is the primary cost driver. Because DuckDB satisfies this query from Parquet footer metadata rather than reading row data, the per-file overhead of opening and parsing each file is the bottleneck. Adding more files increases query time consistently regardless of how many rows those files contain.
For SELECT c5, which is a basic form of column-based querying, row count and data volume have a greater impact on query efficiency compared to transactional row-based query’s. The cost of reading and decompressing a column grows with the number of rows, and per-file overhead is comparatively smaller.
The practical implication is that the optimal Parquet file organization depends on the query pattern. Workloads dominated by lightweight aggregations and metadata queries benefit from fewer, larger files. Workloads involving large column scans across many rows are less sensitive to file count and may benefit from parallelism across multiple files. Neither extreme, one massive file or thousands of tiny files, is universally optimal.

title: Join Algorithms and Join Order in PostgreSQL
text:
Setup
For this simulation, we’ll be using a modified version of PostgreSQL 13.1 which Han et al. (2021) used in their paper on cardinality estimation methods. Use the instructions from their GitHub repository to set up PostgreSQL on your own computer.


Show code
library(DBI)
library(RPostgres)
library(purrr)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)




Show code
con <- dbConnect(
  RPostgres::Postgres(),
  dbname = "stats",
  host   = "127.0.0.1",
  port   = 5432,
  user   = Sys.info()[["user"]]
)


First, we connect to the database. A local PostgreSQL server connection is ideal for this simulation as it will have the least performance variability.
We’ll be using STATS-CEB, an anonymized dump of user-contributed content from the  Stats StackExchange Network , a discussion board featuring questions about various topics in statistics. This database contains 8 tables: tags, comments, badges, posts, users, postLinks, postHistory, and votes. These tables are connected, all by primary keys such as post ID and user ID. For this simulation, a simplified version provided by Han et al. (2021) and included in the paper’s GitHub repository acts as a helpful benchmark for query performance assessment. Follow the instructions in the README for downloading PostgreSQL and the dataset.
For a more in-depth tutorial on setting up a SQL connection in R, see the Data Upload Tutorial.

Indexing the Tables
First, we need to create indexes on the columns involved in the joins we’re performing. This ensures that the nested loop algorithm will be as efficient as possible. For more information on how indexes are structured in SQL, see the  indexes and b-trees page .


Show code
dbExecute(con, "CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_votes_postid       ON votes(postid)")
dbExecute(con, "CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_posts_owneruserid  ON posts(owneruserid)")
dbExecute(con, "CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_badges_userid      ON badges(userid)")




Simulation Settings
In PostgreSQL, session variables can be specifically controlled to simulate different optimizer decisions.

enable_nestloop, enable_hashjoin, enable_mergejoin — turn each physical algorithm on or off. By enabling exactly one at a time, we force the planner to use that strategy.
join_collapse_limit / from_collapse_limit — when set to 1, the planner is forbidden from reordering the tables and must join them in the literal order written in the FROM clause. This is what lets us dictate join order.
max_parallel_workers_per_gather — set to 0 to turn off parallelism, so timing differences reflect the algorithm and not the number of workers.

A key idea: because join_collapse_limit = 1 makes the engine respect the written order, the same logical query written with its tables in a different sequence becomes a different physical plan.


Query Syntax: Good vs. Bad Order
Both queries below answer the exact same question — count the rows produced by joining votes to their posts, those posts to their owners, and those owners to their badges, keeping only upvotes (votetypeid = 2) on well-scored posts (score > 10) owned by high-reputation users (reputation > 1000). They return identical results. The only difference is the order the tables are listed in.


Show code
sql_good_order <- "
  SELECT COUNT(*)
  FROM votes v
  JOIN posts  p ON v.postid = p.id
  JOIN users  u ON p.owneruserid = u.id
  JOIN badges b ON b.userid = u.id
  WHERE v.votetypeid = 2
    AND p.score > 10
    AND u.reputation > 1000
"

# Bad order: large/unfiltered tables first
sql_bad_order <- "
  SELECT COUNT(*)
  FROM badges b
  JOIN users  u ON b.userid = u.id
  JOIN posts  p ON p.owneruserid = u.id
  JOIN votes  v ON v.postid = p.id
  WHERE v.votetypeid = 2
    AND p.score > 10
    AND u.reputation > 1000
"



Good order leads with votes and posts, where the WHERE filters are most selective, so the very first join already works on a much smaller set of rows.
Bad order leads with badges and users, the large tables which are never filtered, so early intermediate results stay huge.

To verify this result, we can run both of the queries separately.


Show code
dbGetQuery(con, sql_good_order)


    count
1 5854758


Show code
dbGetQuery(con, sql_bad_order)


    count
1 5854758

title: Join Algorithms and Join Order in PostgreSQL
text:
Running the Simulation

RUN_REPEATED
To get a stable measurement we run one throwaway query first. This loads the relevant data pages into PostgreSQL’s buffer cache (a warm-cache measurement). Then, we run the query n more times, scraping the Execution Time and Planning Time lines out of each query plan.
EXPLAIN ANALYZE is the tool doing the real work here: it actually executes the query and reports how long each step took, as opposed to plain EXPLAIN, which only prints the query plan.


Show code
run_repeated_warm <- function(con, query, n = 10) {

  # Warm-up run: loads pages into cache, result discarded
  dbGetQuery(con, paste("EXPLAIN ANALYZE", query))

  # Timed trials
  map(1:n, function(i) {
    lines <- dbGetQuery(con, paste("EXPLAIN ANALYZE", query))[[1]]

    exec_time <- lines[grep("Execution Time", lines)] |>
      str_extract("[0-9.]+") |> as.numeric()

    plan_time <- lines[grep("Planning Time", lines)] |>
      str_extract("[0-9.]+") |> as.numeric()

    tibble(run = i, exec_ms = exec_time, plan_ms = plan_time)
  }) |>
  list_rbind()
}




Running all six combinations
We now cross the two join orders with the three join strategies, forcing one strategy at a time by toggling the enable_* flags. That gives six timing distributions of ten runs each.

Nested Loop
First, we enable the nested loop and disable hash and merge join algorithms.


Show code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_nestloop  = on")
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_hashjoin  = off")
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_mergejoin = off")


We’ll run the query once to warm the cache, then extract the results of the next 10 repetitions.


Show code
results_nest_good <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_good_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Nested Loop", order = "good")
results_nest_bad  <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_bad_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Nested Loop", order = "bad")


Now, we’ll go through the same process for hash join.


Show code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_nestloop  = off")
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_hashjoin  = on")
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_mergejoin = off")
results_hash_good <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_good_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Hash Join", order = "good")
results_hash_bad  <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_bad_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Hash Join", order = "bad")


Same for merge join:


Show code
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_nestloop  = off")
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_hashjoin  = off")
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_mergejoin = on")
results_merge_good <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_good_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Merge Join", order = "good")
results_merge_bad  <- run_repeated_warm(con, sql_bad_order, 10) |>
  mutate(join_type = "Merge Join", order = "bad")

dbExecute(con, "DISCARD ALL")





Results
Here’s a plot comparing execution times between different join orders and algorithms.


Show code
bind_rows(
  results_nest_good, results_nest_bad,
  results_hash_good, results_hash_bad,
  results_merge_good, results_merge_bad
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = join_type, y = exec_ms, color = order)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title    = "Effect of join strategy on execution time",
    subtitle = "4-table join: votes, posts, users, badges",
    x        = "Join Strategy",
    y        = "Execution time (ms)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()





Warm-cache execution time across the six strategy × order combinations.




What the plot shows. Each strategy responds differently to a bad join order. A Nested Loop is the most order-sensitive: for every row from the outer side it probes the inner side, so if the outer side starts large (bad order) the probe count explodes. A Hash Join builds a hash table on one input and streams the other past it, which makes it far more forgiving of a bad starting order. A Merge Join requires both inputs sorted on the join key, so its cost is dominated by sorting and it sits somewhere in between. In short: a good join order helps every strategy, but it rescues the Nested Loop.


Reading Query Plans
To understand why some queries take longer than others, it can be helpful to read the plans created by the query optimizer. Wrapping a query in EXPLAIN ANALYZE allows us to read the query plan and see exactly how long each step of the query takes.
Before unpacking the query plans themselves, it’s important to recognize that they are most easily read from bottom to top. Each node/line pulls on the results from the child nodes indented below it. In other words, even though it initializes the query plan, the step labeled “1” in the plan actually finishes last. Additionally, the last 3 lines are summary statistics describing the number of heap fetches (when an Index Only Scan has to go back to the table because the index alone is insufficient), the planning time, and the execution time of the query.
Before unpacking the query plans themselves, it’s important to recognize that they are structured as trees and most easily read from bottom to top. Each node pulls rows from the child nodes indented beneath it, so the leaf nodes produce the first rows and the topmost node — labeled “1” — is the last to finish, even though it’s the first one called. (A few node types, like Hash and Sort, are exceptions in that they must consume their entire input before passing anything up, but the overall direction of reading still holds.) Finally, the last two lines report the planning time and execution time for the whole query, and any Heap Fetches line belongs to an Index Only Scan directly above it — it counts the times that scan had to return to the table because the index alone couldn’t confirm a row’s visibility.

Auto-Optimized Join
First, we’ll run the query and allow the engine to optimize freely.


Show code
auto_plan <- dbGetQuery(con, paste("EXPLAIN ANALYZE", sql_bad_order))[[1]]

auto_exec <- auto_plan |> str_subset("Execution Time") |>
  str_extract("[0-9.]+") |> as.numeric()
auto_plan_time <- auto_plan |> str_subset("Planning Time") |>
  str_extract("[0-9.]+") |> as.numeric()

auto_plan  # print the plan for the reader


 [1] "Aggregate  (cost=2904.66..2904.67 rows=1 width=8) (actual time=1330.417..1330.418 rows=1 loops=1)"                                                
 [2] "  ->  Nested Loop  (cost=805.26..2898.52 rows=2457 width=0) (actual time=12.625..1084.898 rows=5854758 loops=1)"                                  
 [3] "        Join Filter: (u.id = b.userid)"                                                                                                           
 [4] "        ->  Nested Loop  (cost=804.97..2858.88 rows=71 width=8) (actual time=12.605..64.835 rows=45869 loops=1)"                                  
 [5] "              ->  Hash Join  (cost=804.55..2773.90 rows=25 width=12) (actual time=12.548..23.868 rows=2287 loops=1)"                              
 [6] "                    Hash Cond: (p.owneruserid = u.id)"                                                                                            
 [7] "                    ->  Seq Scan on posts p  (cost=0.00..1959.70 rows=3676 width=8) (actual time=0.016..10.144 rows=3552 loops=1)"                
 [8] "                          Filter: (score > 10)"                                                                                                   
 [9] "                          Rows Removed by Filter: 88424"                                                                                          
[10] "                    ->  Hash  (cost=801.06..801.06 rows=279 width=4) (actual time=12.518..12.519 rows=305 loops=1)"                               
[11] "                          Buckets: 1024  Batches: 1  Memory Usage: 19kB"                                                                          
[12] "                          ->  Seq Scan on users u  (cost=0.00..801.06 rows=279 width=4) (actual time=0.008..12.375 rows=305 loops=1)"             
[13] "                                Filter: (reputation > 1000)"                                                                                      
[14] "                                Rows Removed by Filter: 40020"                                                                                    
[15] "              ->  Index Scan using idx_votes_postid on votes v  (cost=0.42..3.34 rows=6 width=4) (actual time=0.003..0.016 rows=20 loops=2287)"   
[16] "                    Index Cond: (postid = p.id)"                                                                                                  
[17] "                    Filter: (votetypeid = 2)"                                                                                                     
[18] "                    Rows Removed by Filter: 3"                                                                                                    
[19] "        ->  Index Only Scan using idx_badges_userid on badges b  (cost=0.29..0.51 rows=4 width=4) (actual time=0.002..0.013 rows=128 loops=45869)"
[20] "              Index Cond: (userid = p.owneruserid)"                                                                                               
[21] "              Heap Fetches: 0"                                                                                                                    
[22] "Planning Time: 2.041 ms"                                                                                                                          
[23] "Execution Time: 1330.503 ms"                                                                                                                      


Following the rows from the bottom up: PostgreSQL begins with two filtered scans. It scans posts and keeps only the 3,552 rows with score > 10 (line 7, discarding 88,424), and scans users to keep the 305 with reputation > 1000 (line 12), building a hash table on those users (line 10). Those two filtered inputs meet at the hash join (line 5), which matches posts to their owners on owneruserid and emits 2,287 rows. From there the rows flow upward through two nested loops: the first probes votes by index once per post (line 15), growing the result to 45,869 rows, and the second probes badges by index once per user (line 19), ballooning it to 5,854,758. Those rows finally arrive at the Aggregate (line 1), which collapses them into a single count.
Notice that we wrote the bad order in the FROM clause, but with reordering left enabled (join_collapse_limit at its default), the planner ignored our written order entirely. It led with posts and users — the two tables carrying the WHERE filters, and therefore the most selective — and pushed the unfiltered fan-out to the very end. Left to its own devices, the planner reconstructs something close to the good order on its own.
Each node reports actual time=startup..total, where the total is cumulative and measured per loop. Reading these upward shows the cost concentrating in one place: everything through the hash join finishes early, the first nested loop shortly after, and then the top nested loop dominates the runtime. That final node is the whole query. It turns 45,869 input rows into 5,854,758 output rows because each user owns roughly 128 badges (rows=128 loops=45869 on line 19), and pushing those 5.85 million rows through the join filter is where the bulk of the 1331 ms runtime lives.


Manually Defined, Inefficient Join
For comparison, we’ll run the same query, but this time, we’ll force the engine to run nested loop joins on our defined “bad order” without allowing the optimizer to manipulate it.


Show code
dbExecute(con, "SET max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 0")
dbExecute(con, "SET join_collapse_limit = 1")
dbExecute(con, "SET from_collapse_limit = 1")
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_nestloop  = on")
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_hashjoin  = off")
dbExecute(con, "SET enable_mergejoin = off")




Show code
forced_plan <- dbGetQuery(con, paste("EXPLAIN ANALYZE", sql_bad_order))[[1]]

forced_exec <- forced_plan |> str_subset("Execution Time") |>
  str_extract("[0-9.]+") |> as.numeric()
forced_plan_time <- forced_plan |> str_subset("Planning Time") |>
  str_extract("[0-9.]+") |> as.numeric()

forced_plan  # print the plan for the reader


 [1] "Aggregate  (cost=4672.77..4672.78 rows=1 width=8) (actual time=5137.780..5137.781 rows=1 loops=1)"                                                         
 [2] "  ->  Nested Loop  (cost=1.01..4666.63 rows=2457 width=0) (actual time=0.034..4854.670 rows=5854758 loops=1)"                                              
 [3] "        ->  Nested Loop  (cost=0.58..1719.61 rows=867 width=4) (actual time=0.026..1215.462 rows=292650 loops=1)"                                          
 [4] "              ->  Nested Loop  (cost=0.29..1449.36 rows=552 width=8) (actual time=0.019..6.775 rows=12371 loops=1)"                                        
 [5] "                    ->  Seq Scan on users u  (cost=0.00..801.06 rows=279 width=4) (actual time=0.007..3.684 rows=305 loops=1)"                             
 [6] "                          Filter: (reputation > 1000)"                                                                                                     
 [7] "                          Rows Removed by Filter: 40020"                                                                                                   
 [8] "                    ->  Index Only Scan using idx_badges_userid on badges b  (cost=0.29..2.28 rows=4 width=4) (actual time=0.003..0.007 rows=41 loops=305)"
 [9] "                          Index Cond: (userid = u.id)"                                                                                                     
[10] "                          Heap Fetches: 0"                                                                                                                 
[11] "              ->  Index Scan using idx_posts_owneruserid on posts p  (cost=0.29..0.48 rows=1 width=8) (actual time=0.009..0.096 rows=24 loops=12371)"      
[12] "                    Index Cond: (owneruserid = b.userid)"                                                                                                  
[13] "                    Filter: (score > 10)"                                                                                                                  
[14] "                    Rows Removed by Filter: 246"                                                                                                           
[15] "        ->  Index Scan using idx_votes_postid on votes v  (cost=0.42..3.34 rows=6 width=4) (actual time=0.003..0.011 rows=20 loops=292650)"                
[16] "              Index Cond: (postid = p.id)"                                                                                                                 
[17] "              Filter: (votetypeid = 2)"                                                                                                                    
[18] "              Rows Removed by Filter: 2"                                                                                                                   
[19] "Planning Time: 0.211 ms"                                                                                                                                   
[20] "Execution Time: 5137.824 ms"                                                                                                                               


Following the rows from the bottom up: with reordering forbidden (join_collapse_limit = 1), the engine must honor the literal FROM order, so it starts where we told it to — the unfiltered tables. It scans users, keeping the 305 rows with reputation > 1000 (line 5), and for each one probes badges by index (line 8), producing 12,371 rows. That result drives a nested loop into posts (line 11), probed once per badge — 12,371 loops — which grows the result to 292,650 rows. Those in turn drive a nested loop into votes (line 15), probed 292,650 times, fanning out to 5,854,758 rows before the Aggregate (line 1) collapses them to a single count. Every join is a nested loop, and no hash table appears anywhere.
Compare the intermediate row counts to the free plan. There, the engine led with the two filtered tables and kept early results tiny — the first join carried 2,287 rows, the next 45,869. Here, forbidden from leading with the selective filters, the engine carries 12,371 rows out of the very first join and 292,650 out of the second. The same 5.85 million rows arrive at the top in both plans, but the forced order hauls a far larger intermediate result through the back half of the tree, and pays for it one row at a time: the votes index scan (line 15) executes across 292,650 loops rather than the 2,287 loops of the free plan. The slowdown shows up directly in the totals — execution time rises from roughly 1331 ms to 5138 ms (line 20), a 3.9-fold penalty for a query returning the identical single number.
This is the worst case the earlier boxplots only hinted at — a bad order and the most order-sensitive algorithm, combined, on a query the planner would otherwise have rescued. The top join still emits the same 5.85 million rows it always did, and the planner still expected only 2,457; what changed is that we stripped away its two best safeguards — the freedom to lead with the selective tables, and the option to use a hash join where a nested loop explodes. Left alone, it used both. Forced, it can use neither.

title: About the Authors
text:
Charlie Coleman
As of June 2026, I’m between my junior and senior years at Pomona College. I’m pursuing a double major in math and art–I work primarily with Professors Jo Hardin and Michael O’Malley. I grew up and went to school in Wellesley, Massachusetts, and I currently live in San Diego. When I’m not in Estella (Pomona’s math building) or at the studio (check out my work here), I love to cook, surf, and play guitar.


Sebastian Pollack
Hi, I’m Sebastian, a rising sophomore at Pomona College studying mathematics, computer science, and German. I grew up in San Carlos, California in the San Francisco Bay Area. Outside of academics, I play violin and viola, enjoy cooking, and love to play and watch soccer.
